schools.summary: The allegory: man = God the Father, vineyard = house of Israel, hedge = Law/angels, winefat = Law/altar, tower = the temple, husbandmen = the priests, the 'far country' = God leaving them to free will (Pseudo-Jerome, Bede).
The parable follows the silencing of the tempters, aimed at the chief priests who knew it (Matt 21:33); Lightfoot's rabbinic detail of the vineyard the Jews boasted to be.
Henry: Christ now shows he will lay aside the Jewish church; church members are God's tenants with a good Landlord, the richly furnished vineyard leaving them without excuse.

The 'servant' = the prophets (a set, not one; Matthew has the plural); Mark's vivid 'one and then another' vs Matthew's two groups; 'at the season' = the fourth-year holy fruit (Lev 19:23-25); the rent is the fruit of obedience (Isa 5:2).
Henry: God was not hasty or high in his demand, sending only at the season for the fruit that was his due.
Gill: 'they caught him' marks the rudeness with which Israel used the prophets — seized, beaten, sent back empty without the fruit.
Bede/Theophylact: the first servant figures Moses, or the prophets of Elijah's time (Micaiah beaten by Zedekiah).
Henry: the base usage God's ministers met in all ages — the prophets persecuted by the very church that bore no fruit.

Another company of prophets, worse used — stoned and shamefully handled; the crux 'wounded in the head' (ekephaliōsan, lit. 'sum up', so some render 'made short work of him'); 'cast stones' textually doubtful (Ellicott).
Bede: the second servant figures David and the Psalmists, despised and rejected ('What portion have we in David?').
The third servant and 'many others' = the whole band of prophets, now killed; the distributive 'beating some, killing some' (Robertson); the slain prophets attested (Matt 23:37; Neh 9:30; Jer 7:25).
Bede/Theophylact: the band of the prophets (of Daniel and Ezekiel's time), none of whom escaped persecution.
Henry: the progression — beaten, wounded, at last killed — traces the husbandmen's hardening, met by the Owner's marvellous patience.
The beloved Son, the householder's last resort; 'beloved son' echoes the baptism (Mark 1:11); 'wellbeloved' and 'last' are Mark's additions; the Son and heir, not a servant (Heb 1:1-2).
Theophylact/Pseudo-Jerome/Bede: 'they will reverence my son' implies no ignorance in God but what they ought to have done — spoken in irony, leaving free will to man.
Henry: the summit of grace, the Father sending his beloved as Jacob sent Joseph (Gen 37:14) — the greater kindness, the greater coming crime.
The rulers plot in conscious guilt, acknowledging him the heir/Messiah; Mark's reflexive 'among themselves' (cf. Matt 21:38); the motive to keep their power and revenues.
Bede: they crucified the Son not through ignorance but envy, knowing he was the heir of Psalm 2:8.
Henry: they killed him to engross all church power, preferring the world's inheritance to the heavenly.

The arrest, delivery to the Gentiles, and crucifixion; Mark's order 'killed then cast out' (Matthew/Luke reverse); 'cast out of the vineyard' = crucified without the gate (Heb 13:12), the deed the Jews' own though by Roman hands.

themes: arrest and crucifixion of the Son

schools.summary: The Lord comes to reckon; in Mark the destruction is part of the parable (in Matthew, the bystanders' answer); fulfilled in Jerusalem's fall by the Romans; the vineyard given to the Gentiles/apostles (Acts; Col 1:6; Jer 17:3).
Henry: nothing more provoking; God will have the rent from another people, being no loser by any.

themes: the vineyard given to the Gentiles

schools.summary: The citation of Psalm 118:22 to teachers who ought to have read it (cf. Matt 21:42); Lightfoot notes the Targum read it of 'the child'.
Bede: the husbandmen are now 'builders', the rulers who should have built God's house but rejected the stone; Theophylact: the corner-stone is Christ joining Jews and Gentiles into one Church.
The continued citation: the exaltation of the once-rejected Messiah is wholly God's doing ('from the Lord', the feminine agreeing with the corner-stone, a Hebraism).
Theophylact: 'marvellous in our eyes' = in the eyes of the faithful; the Church rests on wonders, while the faithless detract.
Henry: it is the Lord's doing to exalt Christ, and his doing to set up Christ's throne in our hearts — a marvel to faith.
The rulers (Mark 11:18) knew the parable was against them (Matt 21:45) and sought to seize him, restrained only by fear of the people (John 7:30); they left and went their way.
Wesley: a wonderful providence — usually the people are restrained by fear of rulers, here the rulers by fear of the people.
Henry: they withdrew lest they be converted and healed; prejudice unconquered by truth is only confirmed.

themes: restrained by fear of the people

schools.summary: The chief priests send Pharisees and Herodians (opposite parties united against Christ, cf. Mark 3:6) to 'catch' (agreuō, hunt/snare) him in his words (Matt 22:15).
Bede: they work through earthly powers to seem guiltless; the question aims to make him deny the tribute so the Herodians can charge sedition; Theophylact: a dilemma with a precipice on both sides.

themes: to catch him in his words

schools.summary: The honeyed flattery ('Master... thou art true... regardest not the person of men', a Hebrew idiom) meant to commit him to fearless speech; the question framed to ensnare whichever way answered.
Pseudo-Jerome/Theophylact: bees with honey in the mouth and a sting in the tail, seeking to deceive Christ by flattery.
Henry: their praise condemns them — if true, they sinned against knowledge; if false, they lied and flattered with the tongue.

themes: Is it lawful to give tribute?

schools.summary: Mark's sharp repeated question ('shall we give or not give?'); 'knowing their hypocrisy' (Mark's specific word vs Matthew's 'wickedness', Luke's 'craftiness') — they did not deceive Jesus; 'why tempt ye me?' and the call for a penny turn the trap.
Henry: hypocrisy cannot be hid from Christ; he ensnares them by their own words, obliging them to own the Roman coin they questioned.
The Roman denarius (~7.5d), bearing Caesar's image and inscription; Lightfoot distinguishes the Caesarean from Jerusalem money, silver from gold; using such coin confessed Caesar's authority.
Bede: Christ asked whose the image was not from ignorance but 'by an economy', to return a fitting answer.
Render to Caesar what bears his image (the coin) and to God what is his (Rom 13:7; 1 Pet 2:17); Caesar may command the money, not the conscience; they marvelled (Mark's imperfect).
Bede: 'things that are God's' = tithes and offerings; Pseudo-Jerome: render yourselves to God, whose image (Ps 4:6), not Caesar's, is stamped on us.
Henry: our hearts and best affections are due to God as rent to a landlord; many admire the wit of the answer but render God nothing.

themes: render to Caesar and to God

schools.summary: The Sadducees deny resurrection, angel, and spirit (Acts 23:8), contradicting Scripture and God's power; after the others' failure they come to display their scepticism and score against Jesus (Robertson).
Henry: the 'deists' of the age — sceptics, not persecutors — attacking his doctrine of the resurrection and the world of spirits.
Pseudo-Jerome allegorises the seven-husbanded woman as the synagogue, deserted by the sevenfold Spirit, leaving no seed of Abraham (Christ given to the Gentiles).
The levirate law (Deut 25:5; Gen 38:8) to raise up seed for a childless brother; the Sadducees accept the Scriptures, especially Moses, adhering to the literal sense and rejecting rabbinic tradition.
Henry: they quote the ancient law to build a case, turning a good statute into a weapon against the resurrection.

themes: common ground turned to a snare

schools.summary: Gill: the seven brethren are supposed of one father's family, such as the law bound; a constructed case, not impossible (Bede), pressing the levirate law to an extreme.
Henry: the number seven multiplies the difficulty, designed both to ridicule the Mosaic law and chiefly to expose the resurrection as absurd.

themes: the snare built brick by brick

schools.summary: Gill: each brother in turn was bound by the levirate law (Deut 25:5) to take the widow; the rabbinic canons regulated such succession, so the tale ran along recognisably lawful lines.
Henry: the accumulation of husbands is essential to the Sadducees' aim — to make the woman's place in a risen life appear an insoluble absurdity.

themes: faithfulness to Moses building the difficulty

schools.summary: The case completed — seven husbands, no seed, the woman dead last (eschaton pantōn); every detail set for the question.
Henry: the Sadducees feign to accept the resurrection while framing a case meant to give it 'a fatal stab' — seven husbands and no heir making the risen woman's place seem absurd.
The question 'whose wife shall she be?'; 'when they shall rise' textually doubtful (Gill, Clarke); the snare assumes a risen life merely continuing this one's relations.
Henry: the Sadducees feign acceptance to give the truth a 'fatal stab' — a baited question whose false assumption Christ will expose.

Christ's double charge: they err (planaomai, wander astray) from ignorance of the Scriptures and of God's power; the posing sceptics ignorant on this very point (1 Cor 15:34); the two ignorances go together.
Henry: they misread Scripture and would not apply God's omnipotence — the power that made soul and body can raise them (Rom 4:21); right knowledge of Scripture is the best preservative against error.

The risen neither marry nor are given in marriage but are 'as the angels' (Luke: equal to angels, cannot die) — the relation made for a dying world ceases; flesh and blood inherit not the kingdom (1 Cor 15:50; Ps 17:15; Luke 20:36).
Theophylact: a heavenly, angelic restoration without decay, so marriage (now for succession against decay) ceases.
Henry: the Sadducees measured the world of spirits by the world of sense; the marriage bond of the earthly paradise is not known in the heavenly.

themes: the bond for a dying world ceases

schools.summary: Christ proves the resurrection from Moses, whom the Sadducees prized; 'at the bush' = citation by section-heading (Exod 3:6); the force lies in the present tense 'I am' the God of the patriarchs.
Cyril of Jerusalem: to those who allow only the Law, the proof is 'I am the God of Abraham' — of those who have being and subsistence.

themes: proof from the book of Moses
'I am' the God of the patriarchs
meeting the Sadducees on their ground

schools.summary: God of the living, not the dead: the patriarchs live in soul and must rise in body, or God is not the God of their whole persons (Wesley's full argument); 'ye greatly err' (Mark only), a solemn yet kindly close.
Henry: God's covenant relation to Abraham proves the soul lives and the body must rise — the Sadducees who deny it greatly err and ought to be told so.

A scribe, hearing Christ answer the Sadducees well, asks the first (chief, not first-in-order) commandment; Matthew says 'tempting', Mark puts him favourably; a standing rabbinic debate over the greatest precept (Lightfoot, Bede).
Augustine: though he came as a tempter he was corrected, or the 'temptation' was honest caution to try an unknown thing.
Henry: his civil application became him — desiring instruction, not tempting; 'first' = first in weight, the foundation of all obedience.

debates.question: Did the scribe come sincerely or to tempt Christ?

sides.position: As a tempter, but corrected by the answer

schools.summary: Christ prefixes the doctrinal foundation — the Shema (Deut 6:4), Israel's daily confession of God's unity ('the Lord our God is one Lord'), which Mark gives more fully than Matthew.
Wesley: from the unity of God (one essence, three persons) it follows we owe all our love to him alone.
Henry: belief that he is one Lord grounds whole-hearted love — he has sole right to us, and so should have sole possession.

themes: the Lord our God is one

schools.summary: Love God with the whole man (Deut 6:5); 'with all thy strength' = the whole capacity of understanding, will, and affections.
Theophylact: the clauses inventory the soul's powers — the living (soul), natural (heart), and rational (mind) — all given whole to God.
Henry: love is the leading affection, the commanding principle that disposes to every duty; Gill: the command stands though nature is fallen, and love is produced in regeneration.

themes: love God with the whole man

schools.summary: The second 'like' the first: love neighbour as self (Lev 19:18; Rom 13:9), comprising the whole duty to man; the two together fulfil all the law (Matt 22:40); 'like' omitted in better MSS.
Theophylact: the two are harmonious and mutually contain each other — he who loves God loves his chief creature, man.
Henry: love the neighbour as truly as oneself, on the ground of shared nature; obedience to these two carries all other duties of course.

The scribe's reply is peculiar to Mark (against the 'abridgment' theory); 'Well, Master, thou hast said truly' (echoing v.14 but now sincere), with real reverence ('Rabbi'), confessing one God and none beside (Deut 4:39).
Calvin: he alone of the questioners was softened, candidly assenting — not of the incurably obstinate; the seed of knowledge choked in his heart drawn out by Christ's word.
The scribe ranks love (intelligent, with the understanding) above all sacrifices — the prophetic truth of 1 Sam 15:22; Hos 6:6; Micah 6:6 — with the fervour of conviction.
Calvin: worship of God is preferable, but its outward exercises must not swallow brotherly kindness; it is 'naked and empty' sacrifices, hypocritical piety, that are ranked below love.
Henry: love must be intelligent; the sacrifices only represented atonement and led to obedience — love is the substance they shadowed.

The scribe answered 'discreetly' (nounechōs, with his wits); 'not far from the kingdom' brings him to its threshold (contrast the lawyer of Luke 10:27); the debate closed — no man durst ask more (Matt 22:46), Christ complete victor.
Theophylact/Bede: not 'within' but 'not far' — near because he favoured the gospel perfection of the two commandments.
Calvin: Christ assists every beginning of docility, encouraging us to proceed; yet the silenced foes only 'groaned within', their obstinacy unbroken.

The silenced foes now questioned; not a puzzle but the key to his person — the scribes rightly taught Messiah = Son of David (Luke 1:32; John 7:42) but missed that David in Ps 110:1 calls him Lord; both deity and humanity involved.
Theophylact/Bede: they call him Son of David but not Lord, a mere man descended from David; reproved not for 'son of David' but for not believing him the Son of God.
Henry: the scribes taught his royal descent but not his divinity; unworthy of Moses' seat are those who can preach truth but not defend it.

themes: Son of David and David's Lord

schools.summary: Psalm 110:1, which Mark emphatically ascribes to the Holy Ghost (more than Matthew/Luke; 2 Pet 1:21; 2 Sam 23:2); 'The Lord said to my Lord' — David owns the Messiah as Lord, exalted to the right hand till his enemies be subdued.
Theophylact: David called him Lord in the Holy Spirit; the enemies made his footstool prove his Lordship.
Cyril of Jerusalem gathers the apostolic witness to Christ's session at God's right hand (Peter, Paul, the Gospel).

themes: David spoke by the Holy Ghost
the Lord said to my Lord

schools.summary: The dilemma — David calls him Lord, how is he his son? — answered only by his divine sonship; 'the common people heard him gladly' (Mark only): the Sanhedrin's attack a colossal failure, the crowds drawn closer.
Bede: the Jews reproved not for 'son of David' but for not believing him Son of God — he is David's son by flesh, David's Lord by deity.
Henry: they heard gladly as a lovely song (Ezek 33:32) yet did not believe — gladness is not faith; some may have later cried 'Crucify him'.
'Beware of the scribes' (blepete apo, vernacular idiom) — Mark's summary of the long indictment of Matthew 23; the marks of pride: love of long robes and of salutations in the marketplaces.
Bede: it is the intention, not the office, that is blamed; the faithful must neither be seduced by their hypocrisy nor moved to imitate their love of praise.
Wesley: the warning was needful and pointed, but no precedent for us, who lack the same authority.

The chief seats in the synagogues (prōtokathedrias, marks of piety; Luke 11:43) and the uppermost rooms at feasts (prōtoklisias, places of honour) — the craving for precedence; even the disciples fell to it (Luke 22:24).
Bede: the seats were lawful and might rightly fall to men of station; the sin is loving and seeking them to feed pride — the intention, not the office.
Henry: they coveted applause, the best seats pleasing a vain fancy as tokens of the value others set on them.

They devour widows' houses, exploiting the helpless under pretence of counsel, with long prayers as the mask (prophasei, pretext); a new sentence ('they that devour... these shall receive'); the greater/more abundant damnation (2 Tim 3:6).

Bede: they take money from the conscience-troubled as feigned advocates — passing the night in prayer to rob the poor; Theophylact: posing as protectors of widows, their doom greater than other sinners'.
Henry: iniquity disguised with piety is double iniquity, doubly doomed — the hypocrite's the greatest damnation; yet sincere long prayers are not discredited by the abuse.
Christ sits over against the treasury (gazophylacium, the temple chests; John 8:20; 2 Kings 12:9) to watch the giving; the rich cast in much; a custom for priests, poor, and widows.

Bede allegorises: the rich = Jews puffed with law-righteousness presuming on themselves; the widow = the Church, poor in casting off pride, widowed because Christ died for her, giving her whole living as of grace.

themes: the heart weighed, not the sum

schools.summary: A destitute widow (ptōchē, pauper) gives two mites (lepta, 'thin', ~2/5 cent), explained by the Latin 'farthing' (quadrans, one of Mark's Latinisms); the pointed contrast with the scribes who devoured widows' houses (vv.38-40).

He calls the disciples to teach a non-quantitative standard; 'more than all' = more than all the rich together — not in amount but in love, self-denial, and sincerity, the rule of God's reward (2 Cor 8:12).
Calvin: offerings are estimated by the heart's feeling, not apparent value; the poor are encouraged to give cheerfully, the rich reminded that self-denial, not amount, is the chief sacrifice.
Wesley: the Judge of all so judges specious outward actions — the smallest gift from self-denying love is most acceptable.

The rich gave of superfluity, sparing what they kept; she of her deficiency cast all her living (bios, livelihood; Deut 24:6), trusting God (cf. 2 Cor 8:2); the lesson — love is measured by proportion, self-denial, and motive, not amount.
Bede: the Church gives her whole living, knowing even her life is of divine grace, not her own desert.

themes: rebuke and comfort in the mites
